text.title: Managing Roadmap Pressure
text.summary: Four techniques for responding to stakeholder pressure for specific features or dates while maintaining an outcome-based orientation.

rule.statement: Replace date commitments with confidence levels (High / Medium / Low / Exploring). When pressed for dates, provide a confidence level plus the conditions that would promote it to "high."
rule.rationale: Stakeholders accept uncertainty more readily when it is named and bounded. "High confidence for Q2 if discovery completes by end of March" is more credible than a date that will slip silently.

rule.statement: Present options, not THE solution: "We have three approaches to achieving this outcome. Here are the trade-offs." Then ask the stakeholder to choose based on priorities.
rule.rationale: Options framing positions the PM as a strategic partner, not a feature factory. Stakeholders who choose an option own the trade-off — they are less likely to escalate when the deprioritized option is not built.

rule.statement: When presenting a priority order, lead with the data and outcome before the "what." "Churn is our top metric risk this quarter; here is why we are prioritizing retention over new features."
rule.rationale: Feature arguments are subjective; metric arguments are objective. Data-led framing shifts the conversation from "I want X" to "will X move the metric?"

section.title: Show the Why Before the What

rule.statement: Structure every roadmap presentation: outcome we are targeting → evidence it matters → options we considered → what we chose and why → confidence level. Never start with the feature list.
rule.rationale: Starting with the feature list invites feature negotiation. Starting with the outcome invites metric discussion. The latter produces better decisions faster.
